---
title: "EU Campsite Demand Forecasting"
subtitle: "Can data predict whether campsites will be full?"
author: "Nuša Brezovnik Bunderla"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    code-fold: true
    self-contained: true
---

## The problem

**Campsite operators and tourism agencies face a planning challenge:**

- When should we hire more staff?
- How much inventory should we pre-order for peak season?
- Is next July going to be as busy as last year?

> Today, most decisions are made on intuition and last year's memory.
> **Can machine learning do better?**

---

## The data

**Source:** Eurostat — official EU statistics office (CC BY 4.0, free to use)

**What it contains:**
Monthly nights spent at camping grounds across EU countries, 2010 to 2026

| | |
|--|--|
| **Rows** | ~7,500 country-month observations |
| **Countries** | 37 European countries |
| **Period** | 2010 – 2026 |

**Two questions we asked:**

1. Will demand be **Low, Medium, or High** this month? *(classification)*
2. **How many nights** will be spent? *(regression)*

---

## What the data tells us

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Camping is extremely seasonal**

- 70% of all annual nights happen in June–August
- July alone = 27% of the entire year

**COVID-19 left a clear mark**

- 2020 saw a 37% drop in total nights
- Recovery was strong by 2022
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Country size matters enormously**

- France: 1.7 billion nights (2010–2026)
- Luxembourg: far fewer

→ This is why we use **country-specific** demand thresholds, not global ones
:::
::::

---

## Our approach

**A leakage-safe machine learning pipeline:**

```
Raw Eurostat data
      ↓
Clean + fill gaps (forward-fill within country)
      ↓
Engineer features: lag last month, lag last year,
                   seasonal flags, cyclical month encoding
      ↓
Time-based 80/20 split  ← train on past, test on future
      ↓
Four models per task: Dummy → Linear → Decision Tree → XGBoost
      ↓
Honest evaluation on test set only
```

**Key design decision:** We split by time, not randomly.
Lag features use past data — a random split would leak future information.

---

## Results — Classification

**Can we predict Low / Medium / High demand?**

| Model | Accuracy | F1 (macro) | ROC-AUC |
|-------|----------|------------|---------|
| Dummy (always guess most common) | 0.20 | 0.11 | — |
| Logistic Regression | 0.63 | 0.54 | 0.79 |
| Decision Tree | 0.72 | 0.71 | — |
| **XGBoost (our model)** | **0.63** | **0.62** | **0.83** |

> **ROC-AUC of 0.83** means the model correctly identifies a High-demand month
> as busier than a Low-demand month 83% of the time.
> Random guessing would score 0.50.

---

## Results — Regression

**Can we forecast the number of nights spent?**

| Model | R² | Average error (MAE) |
|-------|-----|---------------------|
| Dummy (predict the mean) | -0.00 | 1,830,958 nights |
| Ridge Regression | 0.34 | 878,477 nights |
| Decision Tree | 0.43 | 752,320 nights |
| **XGBoost (our model)** | **0.53** | **658,352 nights** |

> R² of 0.53 means the model explains 53% of demand variance.
> The large absolute errors reflect that France and Germany have peaks
> of 10M+ nights — even a 5% miss is hundreds of thousands of nights.

---

## What drives demand?

**Most important features (XGBoost):**

1. **lag_12m** — same month last year → history repeats
2. **lag_1m** — last month's demand → momentum matters
3. **is_summer** — June/July/August are structurally different
4. **Country identity** — France ≠ Luxembourg, always

**What the model should NOT be trusted for:**

- Countries with fewer than 3 years of data (no reliable lag baseline)
- Predicting unprecedented shocks like COVID-19
- Month-level precision for very large countries

---

## Live demo

**Try it yourself:**

→ The app is deployed at: `YOUR_APP_URL`

Select a country, year, and month — the model instantly returns:

- Demand level (Low / Medium / High) with confidence scores
- Estimated nights spent
- Historical trend chart for the selected country

**Built with:** Python · scikit-learn · XGBoost · Streamlit · Eurostat API

---

## Key takeaways

::: {.incremental}
- Campsite demand is **highly predictable in direction** — the model correctly classifies
  demand level with ROC-AUC of 0.83
- **Last year's same month** is the single strongest predictor (lag_12m)
- The model is **useful for planning** (direction and rough magnitude) but not for
  precise night-count targets in large countries
- **COVID showed the limits:** no lag-based model survives a true external shock —
  human judgment remains essential for black-swan events
:::

> *This model gives campsite operators an early-warning signal, not a guarantee.*
